cataloging_subject.label: Trentalle Sancti Gregorii
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English B-version of the Trental of Saint Gregory as represented by the approximately 1480 copy in National Library of Scotland Adv.MS.19.3.1, ff. 213r-216r. Bülbring numbers this witness through line 190, including two displayed positions for wholly missing lines; the separately indexed two-line scribal saying after the explicit and Bülbring's German commentary are represented as scoped source components, not absorbed into the medieval poem's authorship, language, form, or extent. Other B-version witnesses, other Trental recensions, the physical codex, editions, encodings, and facsimiles remain separate layers.

catalog_summary.title: Trentalle Sancti Gregorii
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: By the first half of the fifteenth century; exact composition date unresolved; represented witness c. 1480
catalog_summary.region: England; exact place of composition and dialect region unresolved

titles.value: Some time in Rome a pope there was
notes: Normalized discovery form; the represented witness reads 'Some tyme in Rome a pope þer was'.

scope.description: Explicit of the represented Adv.MS.19.3.1 version

agents.name: Karl Daniel Bülbring
notes: Editor and direct transcriber of the 1891 immediate source edition; not the medieval author.

assertion.notes: The title invokes Gregory and the narrative makes him a character; neither fact establishes Gregory as author.

date_statements.display: By the first half of the fifteenth century; exact composition date unresolved

assertion.notes: The terminus comes from a B-version witness, not from equating the c. 1480 Edinburgh copy with composition. Gordon calls the Brogyntyn B-version witness c. 1400, while the current NLW catalog dates the codex to the first half of the fifteenth century.

date_statements.display: First half of the fifteenth century (NLW Brogyntyn MS II.6)

scope.description: Broad cultural and linguistic context of the Middle English poem

assertion.notes: The NLS catalog's North Midlands description concerns the codex and principal scribe, while the target poem is an approximately contemporary addition by another hand; the Pricke dialect localization in the Brogyntyn catalog likewise must not be transferred to this Trental without work-specific evidence.

language_statements.label: Latin

scope.description: Latin title and explicit, liturgical terms such as Placebo and dirige, and Amen

notes: Brief embedded and paratextual Latin does not make the poem mixed-form.

form_statements.description: Rhyming couplets (aa). Bülbring's represented version has 190 numbered line positions, including two rows that display wholly missing lines; 188 positions contain verbal text. The following two-line scribal saying and prose commentary are separately scoped source components.

subject_statements.label: Purgatory, masses, and prayer for the dead
Secret sin, confession, penance, and salvation
The restless dead and ghostly return

summaries.value: Pope Gregory's mother, revered for outward holiness, confesses that shame kept her from revealing secret sexual sin and the killing of three children. After death she returns as a tormented, foul-smelling ghost and asks Gregory to perform a prescribed trental of thirty masses, the Office of the Dead, and prayer. When the observance is completed she returns radiant among angels and announces her release into bliss. CME00102 represents Bülbring's 190-line Edinburgh-witness version and his commentary; a following two-line statement about fidelity to the copy is a separately indexed scribal saying, not part of the poem.

extent.description: The XML has two DIV1 sections: the poem division contains one LG with 192 L elements, of which 190 belong to Bülbring's numbered Trental lineation and two belong to a separate scribal saying; the second division contains eleven German commentary P elements. The poem's 190 positions include two dotted rows marking wholly missing lines, so 188 positions contain verbal text. Also encoded are two HEAD, twenty NOTE, six PB, fifty-five MILESTONE, two CLOSER, one TRAILER, one EPIGRAPH, one Q, and 347 HI elements.

content_parts.label: Trentalle Sancti Gregorii
content_parts.summary: The represented Adv.MS.19.3.1 B-version: Gregory's mother confesses, returns from purgatorial suffering, prescribes a trental, and after its performance returns in bliss.

locators.value: DIV1 TYPE='poem', LG, first 190 L elements

notes: The two dotted rows at line positions 100 and 150 are retained in the 190-position count; Bülbring explicitly notes the missing rhyme lines.

content_parts.label: Be it trowe or be it fals
content_parts.summary: A two-line saying asserting fidelity to the copy, written after the Trental explicit and separately indexed by DIMEV as record 762 / IMEV 467.5.

locators.value: p. 308, after line 190 and the explicit

notes: Bülbring's footnote says the accompanying writing is in the same hand; DIMEV v1.0 says the saying is in a different hand from the Trental. Direct inspection of the 1600-pixel image did not settle the conflict.

content_parts.label: Anmerkungen
content_parts.summary: Bülbring's German linguistic and textual notes on selected lines, including comparisons with Turnbull, Kaufmann, Cambridge witness C, Lambeth, and Vernon readings.
notes: The single commentary section contains eleven encoded paragraphs. Editorial German is not a language or prose-form property of the medieval poem.

related_works.title: The Trentale of St Gregory (A-version incipit family)
notes: Kaufmann's A-version and Gordon's A-version discussion must not be conflated with the B-version represented by CME00102.

related_works.title: The Trental of St. Gregory (I-written I find a good story)
notes: A related, separately indexed version; its Garrett witness appears within General Corpus item CME00084.

related_works.title: The Life of Saint Gregory's Mother (later quatrain print version)
notes: Gordon identifies the later print form as a C-version; it is not the couplet B-version represented here.

related_works.title: Be it true or be it false / It is as the copy was
notes: Separately indexed two-line scribal saying copied after three works in Adv.MS.19.3.1; the f. 216r occurrence accompanies but is not part of IMEV 3184.

evidence.summary: Direct evidence for identifiers, source title, Middle English poem, embedded Latin, German editorial material, 190-line Bülbring lineation, separate two-line saying, commentary, notes, pagination, foliation milestones, and typography.

evidence.citation: Reproducible XML structure audit of CME00102.xml.
evidence.summary: Counts 22,434 bytes; 2 DIV1; 2 HEAD; 1 LG; 192 L; 11 P; 20 NOTE; 6 PB; 55 MILESTONE; 2 CLOSER; 1 TRAILER; 1 EPIGRAPH; 1 Q; and 347 HI elements. The first 190 L belong to the Trental lineation and the last two to the separate saying.

evidence.citation: Karl Daniel Bülbring, 'Das Trentalle Sancti Gregorii in der Edinburgher Handschrift,' Anglia 13 (1891): 301-308.
evidence.locator: article pp. 301-308; encoded text pp. 303-308; complete-volume PDF pp. 315-322 / poem PDF pp. 317-320 and commentary pp. 320-322
evidence.summary: Immediate-edition evidence for direct manuscript inspection and transcription, witness locus and historical shelfmark, corrections to Turnbull, comparison with Kaufmann and other witnesses, 190-line version, two missing-line positions, separate post-explicit couplet, German notes, and encoded boundaries.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland, Adv.MS.19.3.1 collection-level item record.
evidence.locator: codex description; Trental f. 213; microfilms and Hardman facsimile
evidence.summary: Official evidence for current and former shelfmarks, c. 1480 date, 216-folio paper codex, dimensions, fascicular structure, target text among approximately contemporary additions by another hand, provenance, microfilms Mf.Sec.MSS.173, 549, and 699, and Hardman's 2000 facsimile.

evidence.citation: National Library of Scotland complete IIIF presentation for Adv.MS.19.3.1 and direct image audit.
evidence.locator: 482 canvases; target canvases 441-447 = ff. 213r-216r; adjacent canvas 448 = f. 216v music
evidence.summary: Confirms the physical target sequence, poem beginning on canvas 441/f. 213r, ending and post-explicit saying on canvas 447/f. 216r, and adjacent Deus creator omnium music on canvas 448/f. 216v. It also corrects the provider canvas labels, which are sequential numbers rather than folio labels.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, records 4979 and 762; IMEV/NIMEV 3184 and IMEV 467.5.
evidence.summary: Versioned evidence for title, normalized incipit, 101-couplet tradition-level description, aa couplet form, four B-version witnesses and their loci, related versions, bibliography, and separate indexing of the f. 216r two-line saying. DIMEV's 101-couplet description is not transferred to the shorter 190-position Edinburgh witness.

evidence.citation: Albert Kaufmann, Trentalle Sancti Gregorii: Eine mittelenglische Legende in zwei Texten (Erlangen and Leipzig: A. Deichert, 1889).
evidence.locator: discussion of version B pp. 19-24; B-version Cambridge text pp. 44-49; notes pp. 50-57
evidence.summary: Historical analysis distinguishing the A and B versions, describing Edinburgh E as a 190-line witness with wholly missing lines 100 and 150, relating E and Cambridge C independently to a lost B antecedent, declining to date B more narrowly than its fifteenth-century witnesses, and reporting that he relied on Turnbull plus J. Clark's collation rather than a reliable direct Edinburgh transcript.

evidence.citation: National Library of Wales, Brogyntyn MS II.6, 'The Pricke of Conscience, &c.'
evidence.locator: Trentalle sancti Gregorii ff. 94v-96; codex date and description
evidence.summary: Official evidence for a B-version witness in a first-half-fifteenth-century parchment codex, its exact ff. 94v-96 locus, anglicana formata hand, restricted original, listed digital version and microfilm, and Martin Connolly's 2011 edition. Its Pricke dialect statement is not evidence for the Trental's place of composition.

evidence.citation: Stephen Gordon, 'Conjuring the Dead in St Erkenwald,' Review of English Studies 77.330 (2026): 286-299.
evidence.summary: Current scholarship explicitly distinguishing the A-version, B-versions such as Brogyntyn MS II.6, and later C-version in print, and situating the Trental's ghost, purgatory, confession, and suffrage motifs within late medieval orthodox encounters with the restless dead.

evidence.citation: General Corpus source-lineage manifest for CME00102.
evidence.summary: Linked graph evidence for the repository-to-U-M-to-Bülbring-to-Adv.MS.19.3.1 path, mediated use of Turnbull and Kaufmann, collateral B-version witnesses, complete cached sources, reproduction avenues, access states, and rights distinctions.

open_questions.question: When and where was the B-version composed, what source or sources underlie it, and how exactly are its four DIMEV witnesses related?
next_steps: Consult Martin Connolly's 2011 Brogyntyn edition and later work-specific scholarship.
Collate all four B-version witnesses directly, keeping witness date and dialect distinct from composition.
Do not promote Kaufmann's lost B antecedent or oral-transmission suggestion from historical hypothesis to confirmed exemplar.

open_questions.question: Was the f. 216r 'Be it trowe' saying written by the Trental scribe or by a different hand?
next_steps: Inspect the physical manuscript or a higher-resolution preservation image under raking or multispectral light if available.
Compare the f. 216r hand with the parallel sayings on ff. 7 and 157v.
Preserve Bülbring's same-hand report and DIMEV's different-hand report as attributed, conflicting claims.

open_questions.question: Can complete, openly reusable color facsimiles of CUL Kk.1.6, BL Harley 3810 Part I, and NLW Brogyntyn II.6 be obtained and mapped precisely?
next_steps: Resolve the NLW handle's current HTTP failure and, if restored, cache the complete codex rather than ff. 94v-96 alone.
Check or request whole-object facsimiles from Cambridge and the British Library before ordering excerpts.
Obtain shelfmark-specific reuse statements before redistribution.

notes: The poem is anonymous. Saint Gregory is a narrative figure and traditional title referent, not a documented author.
Richard Heeg or Heege is the principal scribe of the larger codex, but the NLS catalog places the Trental among approximately contemporary additions by another, unidentified hand.

The XML begins with Bülbring's article title on printed p. 303 and omits the article introduction and detailed Turnbull correction list on pp. 301-302; it retains the poem, post-explicit saying, footnotes, and Anmerkungen through p. 308.
The XML's 192 L elements do not mean a 192-line Trental: 190 are the witness-version lineation, including two dotted missing-line positions, and two are the separately indexed scribal saying.
DIMEV's description of 101 couplets is a record-level statement for the broader IMEV 3184 tradition and must not overwrite the 95-couplet-position / 190-line Edinburgh version.
Bülbring's German commentary and the publication's German title matter are editorial layers; the medieval poem remains Middle English verse with brief embedded Latin.

Complete source caches include the provider's entire 482-canvas NLS image sequence, the complete Anglia volume and OCR, DIMEV v1.0, the complete BSB Kaufmann dissertation object, and Gordon's complete article HTML. The Internet Archive Kaufmann PDF is explicitly partial because its scan omits printed pp. 18-25.
